summary: I was born in Nevada City, California, in 1887. It was my early home before my father passed away when I was very young. In 1897, my mother moved with me and my two sisters from Nevada City to Berkeley, marking the start of a new chapter in my life.

After leaving Nevada City in 1897, I grew up in Berkeley, California, with my mother and sisters. Life there was quite different back then; I recall walking through what we called the 'sewers of Berkeley,' which were really just open ditches. Despite the challenges, Berkeley was home during my youth and formative years.

I attended the University of California, Berkeley, graduating in 1912. During my time there, I was very active on campus, including serving as the business manager for The Daily Californian and playing in the Mandolin Club. The university shaped much of my early adult life and provided valuable experiences and mentors.

While at the University of California, Berkeley, I worked as the business manager for The Daily Californian. This role helped me support myself financially and save money for after graduation. It was an important part of my college experience and taught me valuable skills.

During my university years at Berkeley, I enjoyed playing in the Mandolin Club. It was a memorable part of campus life and allowed me to engage with fellow students beyond academics. Those musical moments remain vivid in my memory even now.

In 1914, I co-founded Blyth, Witter, and Co. alongside Charles R. Blyth, Dean Witter, and George Leib. Charles was a key partner in building what became one of the nation's leading investment banking firms. Working with him was instrumental in shaping my career in finance.

Dean Witter was one of my co-founders in establishing Blyth, Witter, and Co. in 1914. Together with Charles Blyth and George Leib, we built a firm that played a significant role in California's financial development. Dean's collaboration was vital to our success in investment banking.

George Leib joined Charles Blyth, Dean Witter, and me in founding Blyth, Witter, and Co. in 1914. Our combined efforts helped grow the firm into a major player in the investment banking industry. George was an important colleague during those early years.

In 1914, I helped found Blyth, Witter, and Co. with Charles Blyth, Dean Witter, and George Leib. The firm grew to become one of the nation's leading investment banks, especially involved in financing California's public utility industry. We navigated through challenging times including two world wars and the Great Depression, which tested our resilience.

The Great Depression was a defining period during my career in investment banking. I recall traveling to our branch offices and insisting they clear their inventories just before the 1929 stock market crash, which was a stroke of luck that saved our firm from severe losses. It was a time of great hardship, but we managed to survive and continue supporting California's industries.

Just before the 1929 stock market crash, I made sure our branch offices cleared their inventories, which was fortunate because many other firms suffered heavy losses for not doing so. This event was a critical moment in my career and in the history of Blyth, Witter, and Co. It underscored the importance of prudence and foresight in business.

California has been central to my life and career, from my birth in Nevada City to my upbringing in Berkeley and my work financing the state's public utility industry. The growth and development of California during the early to mid-20th century provided many opportunities and challenges that shaped my professional journey.

After retiring, I devoted time to compiling and publishing a major genealogy of the Shurtleff family. My family has always been a profound interest of mine, and this project allowed me to explore and preserve our heritage. It was a meaningful pursuit following a long career in finance.
